_schema: Learning-layer data merged onto compounds by build/parse.js (keyed by compound slug). Every field optional; the page renders only what's present. Keep it accurate — authored pharmacology, never generated. analogy=one-line mental model · mechSteps=numbered plain-English cascade (tag links a target, fx=net effect) · pk=pharmacokinetics for the timeline · selfTest=active-recall Q&A · evi=the contextual evidence chapter (howStrong · whatItShows · effect · whoBenefits · caveats · trials[{finding,ref}]) · tech=the biotech deep-dive (chem · targets[{sym,url,action,affinity,role}] · signaling · adme{absorb,distribute,metabolise,excrete} · pgx · dose · tolerance).

caffeine.analogy: Caffeine is a doorstop jammed into your brain's tiredness sensor. It doesn't add energy — it blocks the signal that says “wind down.”

mechSteps.d: Caffeine's shape closely mimics adenosine — the molecule that piles up while you're awake and tells your brain “time to rest.” That resemblance lets it slot into the very same receptors.
By occupying adenosine's A1 and A2A receptors, caffeine stops adenosine from docking — so the drowsiness signal your brain has been building all day never lands.
With the brake released, dopamine and noradrenaline activity rise. You feel sharper and more motivated, and a given effort feels easier.
It also mobilises fat for fuel and mildly lifts heart rate and metabolism (and may sensitise calcium release in muscle) — why it aids endurance and power.

pk.note: That half-life is why timing matters: a 4pm dose still leaves roughly a third circulating at midnight, blocking the very adenosine you need to fall asleep. It stretches to 9 h+ in slow metabolisers and pregnancy, and shortens in smokers.

selfTest.q: What molecule does caffeine impersonate — and what does blocking it do?
selfTest.a: Adenosine. Blocking its receptors lifts the “drowsiness” brake your brain builds through the day, so you feel alert.

selfTest.q: Caffeine's half-life is ~5–6 hours. Why can an afternoon coffee still wreck your sleep?
selfTest.a: After ~10–12 h a quarter of the dose is still circulating, keeping adenosine's sleep signal blocked right when you're trying to fall asleep.

selfTest.q: Two people take the same dose; one gets a performance boost, one gets jittery and slower. What single gene most likely explains it?
selfTest.a: CYP1A2. Fast metabolisers (AA at −163C>A) tend to benefit; slow metabolisers (C-carriers) clear it slowly and can see blunted or negative effects.

evi.howStrong: Caffeine sits near the top of the evidence pyramid — dozens of randomised controlled trials, pooled into multiple meta-analyses and even an umbrella review (a review of reviews). That's about as strong as sports-nutrition evidence ever gets; this is not a 'one small study' supplement. The catch to keep in mind: most of those trials are acute (a single dose) and run in young, active people, so the everyday, long-term picture is less nailed-down than the headline suggests.
evi.whatItShows: Read across the trials and the pattern is remarkably consistent: a small-to-moderate boost to endurance, strength, power and alertness. 'Small-to-moderate' is the honest framing — it won't transform an untrained beginner, but for a trained athlete a few percent is the gap between a personal best and an ordinary day.
evi.effect: In plain numbers: endurance time-to-exhaustion improves ~11% on average, and strength/power gains cluster around a 2–7% edge. The mental lift is biggest when you're sleep-deprived and shrinks toward zero when you're already well-rested — so caffeine mostly restores performance you'd otherwise lose, rather than adding a brand-new ceiling.
evi.whoBenefits: Habitual non-users and fast CYP1A2 metabolisers see the clearest gains. Slow metabolisers and heavy daily users get a blunted (occasionally negative) effect — both tolerance and genetics move the needle, which is why the 'same coffee, opposite result' between two people is real, not imagined.
evi.caveats: Because the trials skew acute, young and male, treat the numbers as a solid central estimate rather than a personal guarantee — and assume publication bias inflates the average a little. Your own response (genetics, tolerance, sleep debt) can easily swamp the study-average effect.

trials.finding: Umbrella review of 21 meta-analyses: consistent small-to-moderate benefit for muscular endurance, strength, power and aerobic endurance.
trials.ref: Grgic et al., 2020, Br J Sports Med

trials.finding: Endurance time-to-exhaustion improved on the order of ~11% vs placebo across pooled trials.
The performance benefit tracked CYP1A2 genotype — present in fast metabolisers, absent or reversed in slow metabolisers.
trials.ref: Guest et al., 2018, Med Sci Sports Exerc

tech.chem: 1,3,7-trimethylxanthine — a purine alkaloid and methylxanthine (a close cousin of theophylline and theobromine). Its ring closely resembles adenosine, which is what lets it dock into adenosine receptors; unlike adenosine, it's cleared by liver enzymes (chiefly CYP1A2) rather than the adenosine-recycling machinery. Lipophilic, so it crosses the blood–brain barrier and placenta freely and distributes into total body water.

targets.affinity: Ki ≈ 2–20 µM; ordinary intake pushes plasma into roughly the same ~10–40 µM range — so this is the target actually engaged at dietary doses
targets.role: the mechanism that matters in real life

targets.affinity: only at ≳500 µM, far above any dietary dose
targets.role: the textbook 'caffeine raises cAMP' story doesn't apply at the doses people actually take

targets.action: sensitiser → more Ca²⁺ release (the classic experimental RyR agonist)
targets.affinity: mainly at concentrations above those reached in the body
targets.role: may add a small direct muscle effect, but caffeine's ergogenic benefit is mostly central (adenosine antagonism)

tech.signaling: The key node is the striatal A2A–D2 heteromer: A2A receptors sit physically coupled to dopamine D2 receptors on medium spiny neurons, and A2A activation normally suppresses D2. Antagonising A2A therefore disinhibits dopamine signalling — the molecular reason caffeine feels dopaminergic without being a dopamine drug. Meanwhile A1 blockade across cortex and basal forebrain lifts tonic inhibition of cholinergic and monoaminergic neurons → global cortical arousal.

adme.absorb: ~99% oral bioavailability; absorbed along the whole GI tract. Food delays the peak but doesn't reduce total absorption.
adme.distribute: Vd ≈ 0.5–0.6 L/kg; ~36% plasma-protein bound; crosses BBB, placenta and into breast milk.
adme.metabolise: ~95% hepatic via CYP1A2 → paraxanthine (the major, active metabolite that drives lipolysis), plus theobromine and theophylline. This single-enzyme dependence is why CYP1A2 status dominates the response.
adme.excrete: <3% excreted unchanged in urine; the rest as demethylated/oxidised metabolites.

tech.pgx: CYP1A2 activity varies up to ~40-fold between people. The CYP1A2*1F (−163C>A, rs762551) allele defines fast vs slow metabolisers: 'fast' (AA) clear it quickly and tend to get the ergogenic and cardiovascular-neutral profile; 'slow' (C-carriers) show blunted or even negative performance effects and a higher heart-attack signal at high intake. ADORA2A variants (rs5751876) predict caffeine-induced anxiety and sleep disruption. CYP1A2 is induced by smoking and cruciferous vegetables (faster clearance) and inhibited by oral contraceptives and pregnancy (half-life can roughly double).
tech.dose: Ergogenic window 3–6 mg/kg (~200–400 mg) taken 45–60 min pre-effort; cognitive benefit plateaus by ~200 mg. Above ~9 mg/kg there's no extra benefit — only rising tachycardia and jitter. Acute toxicity climbs past ~1.2 g and lethal doses cluster near 10–14 g, a risk driven almost entirely by anhydrous powder (a teaspoon can be a lethal dose).
tech.tolerance: Chronic exposure upregulates adenosine A1/A2A receptor density as compensation, so the net alerting effect fades over ~1–4 weeks and abrupt cessation unmasks the surplus receptors → withdrawal headache and fatigue. A 10–14-day taper every ~2 months restores sensitivity — the pharmacological basis of 'cycling'.
